--- SOURCE 1: The learning problems of design-based interdisciplinary education ---
URL: https://dl.designresearchsociety.org/cgi/viewcontent.cgi?article=1098&context=iasdr

SUMMARY:
The study found that the main challenges and difficulties in design-based interdisciplinary learning included communication, the integration of different disciplines, the lack of students' collaboration skills, learning and cooperation difficulties with regard to different disciplines, and differences in thinking

FULL CONTENT:
This work is licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial 4.0 International Licence. The learning problems of design-based interdisciplinary education Wenzhi Chenab a Chang Gung University, Taoyuan, Taiwan (R.O.C.) b Ming Chi University of Technology, New Taipei City, Taiwan (R.O.C.) wenzhi@mail.cgu.edu.tw doi.org/10.21606/iasdr.2023.289 The primary purpose of this study was to explore the challenges and difficulties of design-based interdisciplinary projects, the learning problems encountered by university students and the learning resources they used to overcome these problems. A total of 247 students participated in the survey. The study found that the main challenges and difficulties in design-based interdisciplinary learning included communication, the integration of different disciplines, the lack of students' collaboration skills, learning and cooperation difficulties with regard to different disciplines, and differences in thinking across disciplines. The problems students encountered included difficulties with interdisciplinary collaboration, inadequate professional guidance, personality clashes of members, a lack of instructors from different disciplines, and professional parochialism and barriers. The primary resources used by students to overcome learning problems included human resources, Internet and economic resources, and time. Keywords: design-based interdisciplinary learning; learning problems; learning resources 1 Introduction Design is becoming increasingly important in enterprise; indeed, there is a focus on design-driven innovation (Battistella et al., 2012; Verganti, 2009) and a move towards design-led educational innovation concepts (Wright & Wrigley, 2017). Integrating knowledge from various disciplines is necessary to solve complex problems, and this idea has led to a push for cross-disciplinary teaching in higher education (Ashby & Exter, 2018; Self et al., 2018). Design education also incorporates cross-disciplinary and interdisciplinary methods to address complex design issues (Norman & Klemmer, 2014; Self & Baek, 2016). Additionally, design thinking, user-centred design and interdisciplinary design have become trends in education due to the complexity of design issues and a growing concern for design education (Kim et al., 2012; Norman, 2005; Self et al., 2018). Interdisciplinary courses are planned and carried out in various forms to accommodate the complexity of design issues (Holley, 2 2017; Self & Baek, 2016; Self et al., 2018). As views about education shift towards a focus on student-centred learning (Watson & Reigeluth, 2008), students' learning problems should be considered. Students often experience learning problems during their education (Chen, 2015). Traditional learning problems about design revolve around solving design problems caused by personal and environmental (as well as other) factors. However, the nature of both the personnel and knowledge involved in interdisciplinary courses and learning becomes more complicated. The participation of teachers from different fields, student learning that has previously focused on other areas, and the introduction of professional knowledge from different fields can complicate the learning process, leading to more complex learning problems among students. 2 Literature review 2.1 Interdisciplinary education Interdisciplinary education can provide students with knowledge and skills that enable them to examine the world from multiple perspectives, integrate various disciplines to better understand the phenomena they explore, and understand the interdependence between disciplines or topics and comprehend the larger systems within which each discipline exists (Fortuin et al., 2013; Styron, 2013). An interdisciplinary learning environment can help students engage in critical thinking and evaluate and integrate the application of knowledge from various disciplines. It can also enhance problem-solving and innovation skills, as well as creativity, and help students develop collaboration and communication skills (Cowden & Santiago, 2015; Mobley et al., 2014; Styron, 2013). Students who participate in interdisciplinary courses learn interdisciplinary approaches via the teaching methods of lecturers; these may include the 'lifelong learning' approach and exploration (Styron, 2013). Embedding interdisciplinary courses into higher education curricula can help students develop the ability to apply knowledge and skills from multiple disciplines to solve problems and find solutions that would not be possible using a single disciplinary lens (Ashby & Exter, 2018). Teaching interdisciplinary courses requires pedagogical support (Madison & Augsburg, 2013). Currently, no teaching method can simultaneously meet the demands of interdisciplinary teaching and learning. Students' prior preparation and previous learning experiences can affect how they learn in interdisciplinary courses (Ashby & Exter, 2018). Richards (1996) pointed out that team-taught courses often fail to achieve their goals because the teaching team members never truly understand what they collectively focus on in an interdisciplinary way. An optimal interdisciplinary learning experience requires close collaboration, good team planning and a teaching team from different fields; such a structure also provides an opportunity to integrate various teaching techniques (Lefeber et al., 2013). 2.2 Learning problems in design-based interdisciplinary education The learning process can affect learning outcomes. Students often encounter various problems when learning design that affect the design process, the learning process, and the results (Chen, 2015). The causes of learning problems are complex and include many elements, such as course organization and planning, topics and subjects, teaching styles and methods, and learning styles (Chang et al., 2000). Knowledge can be divided into four categories: declarative, procedural, schematic, and strategic. With the demand for innovation and rapidly changing and complex problems, learning about and applying these knowledge types has become increasingly important in design education (Self et al., 2018). 3 Interdisciplinary education varies depending on the disciplines involved; it has particular characteristics when applied to education in the design field due to the uniqueness of that discipline. Xu et al. (2022) investigated the efficiency of students' interdisciplinary learning. The study collected data through questionnaires and interviews and found no significant difference in interdisciplinary integration ability between students participating in interdisciplinary learning and those participating in single-discipline learning. The main reasons included insufficient motivation and lack of experience in interdisciplinary learning, personal traits, increased learning pressure and academic burden, and interference from disciplinary factors. Self et al. (2018) explored the driving factors and challenges of interdisciplinary design education through the lens of student learning. The study involved in-depth interviews with students from two interdisciplinary design schools in South Korea and the UK. The findings revealed that both schools recognized the importance of an interdisciplinary perspective. However, the display of interdisciplinary skills largely depended on the students' attitudes and interests and not on the curriculum planning itself. Teacher bias and potential integration problems were identified as obstacles to interdisciplinary learning at both the school and institutional levels. The study also highlighted that applying interdisciplinary skills was a key driver for acquiring and integrating perspectives from various disciplines. Design students responded positively to interdisciplinary learning courses, indicating that they benefited from a broader perspective and more comprehensive skills. In a separate study, Self and Baek (2016) surveyed students' learning experiences in interdisciplinary teaching within design education. The results indicated that students preferred instruction provided by a single, professional teacher; this led to a better learning quality and experience. In contrast, using multiple teachers comprising a teaching team appeared to create more problems. However, the study also suggested that interdisciplinary teaching teams can offer diverse knowledge and perspectives from different disciplines, giving students more opportunities to understand the relationship between different subjects and course topics. Effective teaching strategies are needed to leverage these benefits. For students, traditional design education already involves encountering numerous problems that affect their learning processes and the outcomes of their design work. When introducing interdisciplinary knowledge and skills, professional teachers from different disciplines collaborate with students – who up to that point have studied in different disciplines – to solve cross-disciplinary design problems. Students then face further complex issues that affect their learning processes and outcomes. In light of this, this study aims to explore the learning problems encountered by design students participating in design-based interdisciplinary courses and the learning resources used to solve these problems. 3 Method 3.1 Research questions Research questions were developed to explore students' problems related to design-based interdisciplinary learning, including the source of the problems, the learning resources used and related issues. The main research questions were: 1. What are the challenges and difficulties of design-based interdisciplinary courses and activities? 4 2. What learning problems do students encounter in design-based interdisciplinary courses and activities? 3. What learning resources do students use to help solve cross-field learning problems? 3.2 Participants A total of 274 university students were investigated, including 52 from public universities, 222 from private universities; 43 from general universities, and 231 from technical and vocational universities. There were 89 male and 185 female students. Twenty-four students had never participated in interdisciplinary courses or activities, 105 students had participated once, 59 had participated twice, and 83 had participated 3 (inclusive) times. After removing the students who had not participated in interdisciplinary design-related activities and the missing data, the total number of students who had participated in design-based interdisciplinary courses or related activities was 247. 3.3 Data collection and analysis Data were collected using a questionnaire designed according to a previous qualitative study conducted by the authors (2022) that used a focus-group interview. The main content of the questionnaire centred on 1) the challenges and difficulties of design-based interdisciplinary courses or activities, 2) the learning problems students faced in design-based interdisciplinary courses or activities, 3) the learning resources students used in design-based interdisciplinary courses or activities and 4) basic participant information. The survey was conducted with the approval of the research ethics review and followed the regulations. All the questionnaire data were entered into a Microsoft Excel spreadsheet, then transferred to the Windows version of SPSS statistical software for various descriptive statistics and analysis tests. 4 Results 4.1 Challenges and difficulties The degree of challenges and difficulties related to the interdisciplinary project was investigated with a questionnaire. Table 1 shows the descriptive statistics for the participation experience. The challenge/difficulty that received the highest score was item B23: Maintaining an open mind is important for interdisciplinary collaboration (M = 4.65, SD = 0.599). The second was B01: Professional knowledge and perspectives vary across different disciplines (M = 4.51, SD = 0.680). The third was B22: Acquiring the foundational knowledge of different disciplines is important for interdisciplinary collaboration (M = 4.41, SD = 0.738). The fourth was B25: Collaborative skills have a significant impact on the outcomes of interdisciplinary collaboration (M = 4.38, SD = 0.645). The fifth was B24: Letting go of personal discipline superiority helps with interdisciplinary collaboration (M = 4.31, SD = 0.734). These items' scores exceeded 4.00, indicating that students' characteristics, attitudes and attainment of basic knowledge in different fields play an essential role in interdisciplinary learning. analysis was performed to summarize the factors involved in the participation experience. First, Kaiser-Meyer-Olkin (KMO) and Bartlett tests were conducted. The KMO measure of sampling adequacy was 0.837, which indicated that the data's partial correlation was suitable for factor analysis, as the value was more than 0.8. In addition, Bartlett's test of sphericity yielded a significance level of 0.000 (p < 0.001), indicating that factor analysis could be performed. After conducting a factor analysis on students' experiences participating in interdisciplinary learning courses and activities, seven main factors were identified, with a total cumulative explained variance of 62.476%. Table 2 shows the component matrix of the rotated factors, which reveals the correlation between the seven factors and their component values. Based on the content of the questions, the seven factors were as follows: 1) interdisciplinary communication barriers, 2) interdisciplinary collaboration issues, 3) interdisciplinary integration problems, 4) interdisciplinary collaboration competency issues, 5) interdisciplinary learning barriers, 6) interdisciplinary collaboration difficulties and 7) interdisciplinary thinking differences. 6 These factors are the causes and contributors of learning problems and are related to professional knowledge and skills across different domains, thinking differences, and students' cognition and attitudes. problems Table 3 presents the descriptive statistics for the learning problems. From the table, it can be seen that the item with the highest mean score was C14: Team members' motivation affects the collaboration process and outcome (M = 4.29, SD = 0.677). The second is C15: Team members' personalities affect the project's processes and outcome (M = 4.26, SD = 0.755). The third is C13: Team members' professional abilities affect the project's outcome (M = 4.19, SD = 0.762). These results indicate that the individual cognition and mindset of the members participating in interdisciplinary courses and learning activities play an essential role in interdisciplinary learning activities and are an important indicator of a successful interdisciplinary learning course and activity. It can also be inferred from these results that there is still room for improvement in guiding the learners' cognition and psychological readiness before they embark on current interdisciplinary courses and learning projects. between disciplines. 247 2.98 1.051 To further classify the content of learning problems, Kaiser-Meyer-Olkin and Bartlett tests were conducted on the learning problem items. The Kaiser-Meyer-Olkin measure of sampling adequacy was 0.807, more significant than 0.8, indicating that the data's partial correlations were suitable for factor analysis. Additionally, the significance of Bartlett's test of sphericity was 0.000 (p < 0.001), indicating that factor analysis could be performed. These results show that the data met the conditions for factor analysis. Next, we conducted a factor analysis to summarize the main elements of the learning problems. Learning-problem items were further classified using principal component analysis. According to the results, the learning problems can be placed into six categories, with a cumulative total explained variance of 65.581%. Table 4 presents the component matrix of the learning-problem factors after rotation, with the problems of each factor sorted according to its component score. The main factors were renamed, based on their related problems, as follows: 1) difficulty of interdisciplinary collaboration, 2) insufficient professional guidance, 3) influence of member factors on cooperation and outcomes, 4) lack of interdisciplinary knowledge, 5) insufficient professional diversity and 6) professional egocentrism/barriers. These results indicate that interdisciplinary learning needs to consider members' personalities and professional domains while providing adequate prior training and basic knowledge in relevant fields. projects. 247 4.32 .769 We first conducted Kaiser-Meyer-Olkin and Bartlett tests on the items to classify learning resources. The Kaiser-Meyer-Olkin measure of sampling adequacy was 0.945, indicating that the data's partial correlations were suitable for factor analysis. In addition, the significance of Bartlett's sphericity test was 0.000 (p < 0.001), indicating that factor analysis could be performed. The results showed that these data were suitable for factor analysis. The learning resource items are classified by factor analysis, which can be divided into three factors with a total explanatory power of 61.230%. SUMMARY:

interaction that unfolds between the practitioner and the situations he or she engages. …artistry is possible when both ends and means are open to clarification and shaping by the skills, knowledge and interest of a practitioner in situations where ambiguity, risk, complexity and indeterminacy abound.” solution and then progress by following a predetermined set of instructions.” “…an effort to use personal capabilities and intentions to generate both a desired outcome and to find a way of achieving this outcome with the materials and resources at hand.”  # Using design thinking for interdisciplinary curriculum design and teaching: a case study in higher education

[*Humanities and Social Sciences Communications*](/palcomms)
**volume 11**, Article number: 307 (2024)
[Cite this article](#citeas)

14k Accesses

10 Citations

[Metrics details](/articles/s41599-024-02813-z/metrics)

### Subjects

## Abstract

This study investigates the utilization of design thinking by a university instructor in the development and delivery of an interdisciplinary curriculum. It examines the challenges encountered and the coping strategies employed during the process. The research, conducted as a case study of an interdisciplinary program course at a university in southern Taiwan from September to December 2022, involved data collection through field observations, in-depth interviews, and document analysis. All data were transcribed verbatim, coded, and triangulated to enhance research validity. The findings suggest that design thinking serves as an effective methodology for interdisciplinary curriculum design and teaching. It emphasizes the integration of practice and application to facilitate students’ interdisciplinary collaboration and co-creation. Additionally, the study evaluates teaching strategies, with a focus on the role of visual tools, communication, and on-site observation. The insights gained from this research provide valuable perspectives on challenges in interdisciplinary teaching, particularly in a post-pandemic context. They guided the development of effective practices in Taiwan and beyond, addressing crucial aspects such as the roles of instructors, resource allocation, and the cultivation of interdisciplinary talent. The study emphasizes the continuous need for adaptation and the prioritization of depth in learning outcomes.

## Introduction

Individuals and industries worldwide have encountered unprecedented challenges and changes in the aftermath of the COVID-19 pandemic. It is crucial for higher education to nurture future professionals capable of effectively addressing increasingly complex societal problems (Berasategi et al., [2020](/articles/s41599-024-02813-z#ref-CR1 "Berasategi N, Aróstegui I, Jaureguizar J, Aizpurua A, Guerra N, Arribillaga-Iriarte A (2020) Interdisciplinary learning at university: assessment of an interdisciplinary experience based on the case study methodology. Sustainability 12:7732. 
                  https://doi.org/10.3390/su12187732
                  
                ")). With the proliferation of intricate global issues such as climate change, unemployment, healthcare, immigration, pollution, and safety (Hardy et al., [2021](/articles/s41599-024-02813-z#ref-CR14 "Hardy JG, Sdepanian S, Stowell AF, Aljohani AD, Allen MJ, Anwar A, Barton D, Baum JV, Bird D, Blaney A, Brewster L, Cheneler D, Efremova O, Entwistle M, Esfahani RN, Firlak M, Foito A, Forciniti L, Geissler SA, Wright KL (2021) Potential for chemistry in multidisciplinary, interdisciplinary, and transdisciplinary teaching activities in higher education. J Chem Educ 98:1124–1145. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1021/acs.jchemed.0c01363
                  
                ")), interdisciplinary approaches have emerged as essential strategies. The surge in complex problems underscores the inevitability of interdisciplinary learning. Higher education, in response to these challenges, strives to cultivate skills that transcend traditional boundaries, including interdisciplinary thinking (Spelt et al., [2009](/articles/s41599-024-02813-z#ref-CR35 "Spelt EJH, Biemans HJA, Tobi H, Luning PA, Mulder M (2009) Teaching and learning in interdisciplinary higher education: a systematic review. Educ Psychol Rev 21:365–378. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1007/s10648-009-9113-z
                  
                ")). Major universities in Taiwan have demonstrated their commitment to promoting interdisciplinary education through mechanisms such as interdisciplinary courses, micro-credit courses, and credit programs. Interdisciplinary education aims to develop students’ boundary-crossing skills, fostering problem-solving, communication, collaboration, critical thinking, entrepreneurship, and innovative practices. The implementation of interdisciplinary cooperation in teaching and learning becomes a key focus in cultivating future talent and enhancing students’ integrated abilities.

Interdisciplinary learning involves transcending the boundaries of a single specific subject or field and providing students with connections and interactions between different subject areas. This interconnectivity helps them to understand the limitations and frameworks of their knowledge or skills and to integrate the knowledge of two or more disciplines to produce a cognitive advancement in ways that would have been impossible or unlikely through single disciplinary means (Spelt et al., [2009](/articles/s41599-024-02813-z#ref-CR35 "Spelt EJH, Biemans HJA, Tobi H, Luning PA, Mulder M (2009) Teaching and learning in interdisciplinary higher education: a systematic review. Educ Psychol Rev 21:365–378. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1007/s10648-009-9113-z
                  
                ")). It cultivates their ability to integrate different knowledge systems in facing complex problems in their careers or society. Moreover, interdisciplinary curriculum teaching significantly impacts instructors and learners, as compared to single-field curriculum teaching (Chang and Lin, [2019](/articles/s41599-024-02813-z#ref-CR6 "Chang C-Y, Lin C-C (2019) Promoting transdisciplinary learning in higher education: trends, myths, approaches and challenges. Curric Instr Q 22(2):31–48. 
                  https://doi.org/10.6384/CIQ.201904_22(2).0002
                  
                "); McLaughlin et al., [2022](/articles/s41599-024-02813-z#ref-CR24 "McLaughlin JE, Chen E, Lake D, Guo W, Skywark ER, Chernik A et al. (2022) Design thinking teaching and learning in higher education: experiences across four universities. PLoS ONE 17(3):e0265902. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0265902
                  
                "); Spelt et al., [2009](/articles/s41599-024-02813-z#ref-CR35 "Spelt EJH, Biemans HJA, Tobi H, Luning PA, Mulder M (2009) Teaching and learning in interdisciplinary higher education: a systematic review. Educ Psychol Rev 21:365–378. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1007/s10648-009-9113-z
                  
                ")). Through interdisciplinary dialog, instructors can learn from others’ experiences or identify their weaknesses, promoting professional growth, enhancing their teaching and curriculum planning abilities, and guiding students to think and cooperate to solve problems. Meanwhile, students have opportunities for multi-faceted exploration, using their knowledge and skills in their professional fields and collaborating with students from other fields. Interdisciplinary education influences students’ learning styles and thinking patterns as they are exposed to different areas of expertise (Chen et al., [2017](/articles/s41599-024-02813-z#ref-CR7 "Chen L-C, Wang T-H, Chiu F-Y, Shen S-Y, Zeng M (2017) Developing the interdisciplinary integration-based core competencies scale: a case study of maternal–infant services curriculum. Chin J Sci Edu 25(2):143–168. 
                  https://doi.org/10.6173/CJSE.2017.2502.03
                  
                ")). Despite these benefits, instructors and learners face challenges in interdisciplinary collaboration. For instructors, the challenge lies in seamlessly integrating the cognitive and ideational aspects of individuals from diverse fields within the classroom, aiming for effective communication (Tsai, [2014](/articles/s41599-024-02813-z#ref-CR40 "Tsai M-L (2014) How to facilitate learning of interdisciplinary collaboration skills. Sci Mon 529:4–5")). For learners, the challenges lie in communication problems arising from the heterogeneity of members’ backgrounds, the ratio of team leaders to experts, how consensus is formed, and the ongoing conceptual analysis and evaluation conducted during the process (Tang and Lin, [2011](/articles/s41599-024-02813-z#ref-CR39 "Tang H-H, Lin Y-Q (2011) The influence and problems of scenario design approach on multi-disciplinary collaboration design. J Design 16(3):21–44. 
                  https://doi.org/10.6381/JD.201109.0021
                  
                ")).

Design thinking (DT) represents a 21st-century skill aimed at generating timely solutions to complex and practical problems (Tan, [2017](/articles/s41599-024-02813-z#ref-CR38 "Tan A-G (2017) Cross-disciplinary creativity and design thinking. In: Darbellay F, Moody Z, Lubart T (eds) Creativity, design thinking and interdisciplinarity. Creativity in the twenty first century. Springer, Singapore")). Therefore, in the post-epidemic era, as the trend to cultivate interdisciplinary talent increases, this case study investigates how a university instructor uses design thinking to design and teach a university interdisciplinary curriculum in the context of a university interdisciplinary program. Specifically, we explore the challenges encountered and coping strategies during the development of the interdisciplinary curriculum and teaching practice, and the professional growth and reflection resulting from the process. Our findings can serve as a reference to promote interdisciplinary education.

## Literature review

### Interdisciplinary teaching models

The term “interdisciplinary” is widely used to encompass a variety of activities related to communication, interaction, and integration among all disciplines. The dismantling of disciplinary boundaries characterizes interdisciplinary learning, and it can provide students with opportunities to engage and interact with different fields of study. The main objective of this approach is to guide students to gain a deeper understanding of the constraints and frameworks of their knowledge or skills while developing their capacity to comprehend and integrate different knowledge systems (Guo, [2008](/articles/s41599-024-02813-z#ref-CR13 "Guo C-J (2008) Introduction to interdisciplinary research and education. Res Innov 7:5–6. 
                  https://doi.org/10.29603/ZHWHGX.200805.0002
                  
                "); Spelt et al., [2009](/articles/s41599-024-02813-z#ref-CR35 "Spelt EJH, Biemans HJA, Tobi H, Luning PA, Mulder M (2009) Teaching and learning in interdisciplinary higher education: a systematic review. Educ Psychol Rev 21:365–378. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1007/s10648-009-9113-z
                  
                ")). Students participating in interdisciplinary programs tend to develop a more holistic perspective and solution-oriented strategies rather than solely gaining content-specific knowledge from a single discipline. Interdisciplinary learning is aimed at cultivating multiple essential professional competencies, to address emerging social and employment challenges (Chang and Lin, [2019](/articles/s41599-024-02813-z#ref-CR6 "Chang C-Y, Lin C-C (2019) Promoting transdisciplinary learning in higher education: trends, myths, approaches and challenges. Curric Instr Q 22(2):31–48. 
                  https://doi.org/10.6384/CIQ.201904_22(2).0002
                  
                "); Ivanitskaya et al., [2002](/articles/s41599-024-02813-z#ref-CR17 "Ivanitskaya L, Clark D, Montgomery G, Primeau R (2002) Interdisciplinary learning: process and outcomes. Innov High Educ 27(2):95–111. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1023/A:1021105309984
                  
                "); Repko, [2008](/articles/s41599-024-02813-z#ref-CR31 "Repko AF (2008) Interdisciplinary research. Sage, Thousand Oaks, CA"); Repko and Szostak, [2021](/articles/s41599-024-02813-z#ref-CR32 "Repko AF, Szostak R (2021) Interdisciplinary research: process and theory, 4th edn. Sage")).

Previously, two inquiry-based teaching approaches, problem-based learning (PBL) and project-based learning (PjBL) were commonly used in interdisciplinary education (Majeski, [2005](/articles/s41599-024-02813-z#ref-CR22 "Majeski R (2005) Interdisciplinary problem-based learning in gerontology: a plan of action. Educ Gerontol 31:733–743. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1080/03601270500250119
                  
                "); Modo and Kinchin, [2011](/articles/s41599-024-02813-z#ref-CR28 "Modo M, Kinchin IM (2011) A conceptual framework for interdisciplinary curriculum design: a case study in neuroscience. J Undergrad Neurosci Educ 10(1):71–79"); Stentoft, [2017](/articles/s41599-024-02813-z#ref-CR36 "Stentoft D (2017) From saying to doing interdisciplinary learning: is problem-based learning the answer? Act Learn High Educ 18(1):51–61"); Wróblewska and Okraszewska, [2020](/articles/s41599-024-02813-z#ref-CR46 "Wróblewska D, Okraszewska R (2020) Project-based learning as a method for interdisciplinary adaptation to climate change—Reda valley case study. Sustainability 12:4360. 
                  https://doi.org/10.3390/su12114360
                  
                "); Yang and Lin, [2015](/articles/s41599-024-02813-z#ref-CR48 "Yang W-C, Lin C-C (2015) Effectiveness of the interdisciplinary team to integrating operation rd system—a study of different team backgrounds of the same department affect contest learning effectiveness. Manag Inf Comput 4(1):285–293. 
                  https://doi.org/10.6285/MIC.4(1).23
                  
                ")). These approaches differ in learning content, problem structure, and learning outcomes. For example, the PBL learning style is more divergent, guiding learners to form different sub-problems by setting out structurally ambiguous problems. On the other hand, PjBL is more convergent, forming sub-problems through perceiving different phenomena or exploring the impact of different variables (Yang et al., [2018](/articles/s41599-024-02813-z#ref-CR47 "Yang C-Y, Kang S-C, Chen Y-F, Lin C-Y, Leng E-S, Lin Y-H (2018) Using “design-based learning” as preliminary foundation of smart aging design course. Chin J Sci Educ 26(S):399–418. 
                  https://doi.org/10.6173/CJSE.201812/SP_26.0002
                  
                ")).

More recently, interdisciplinary project-based learning (IPBL) has emerged. Like the approach businesses use to develop teams in multiple areas, IPBL provides sufficient training for teamwork and collaboration to students from different disciplines, preparing them to engage in complex technical, engineering, educational, and social projects. Students can promote their learning by contributing their strengths and resources (Carpenter et al., [2007](/articles/s41599-024-02813-z#ref-CR5 "Carpenter SL, Delugach HS, Etzkorn LH, Farrinton PA, Fortune J, Utley DR, Virani SS (2007) A knowledge modeling approach to evaluating student essays in engineering courses. J Eng Educ 96(3):227–239. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1002/j.2168-9830.2007.tb00932.x
                  
                "); Johansen et al., [2009](/articles/s41599-024-02813-z#ref-CR18 "Johansen D, Scaff C, Hargis J (2009) Interdisciplinary project-based model for enhanced instruction course. Int J Scholarsh Teach Learn 3(1), Article 22. 
                  https://doi.org/10.20429/ijsotl.2009.030122
                  
                "); Stozhko et al., [2015](/articles/s41599-024-02813-z#ref-CR37 "Stozhko N, Bortnik B, Mironova L, Tchernysheva A, Podshivalova E (2015) Interdisciplinary project-based learning: technology for improving student cognition. Res Learn Technol 23:1–13. 
                  https://doi.org/10.3402/rlt.v23.27577
                  
                "); Whitney, [2014](/articles/s41599-024-02813-z#ref-CR45 "Whitney MC (2014) Interdisciplinary collaboration in the classroom. Int J Interdiscip Stud Commun 8(4):1–8. 
                  https://doi.org/10.18848/2324-7320/CGP/v08i04/53595
                  
                ")). IPBL can help students develop creativity, overcome the barriers of disciplinary self-centeredness, facilitate the development of knowledge management processes, stimulate their interest and motivation in subject curricula, promote their participation in the learning process, and enhance their cognitive levels and satisfaction with learning outcomes (Biasutti and EL-Deghaidy, [2014](/articles/s41599-024-02813-z#ref-CR2 "Biasutti M, EL-Deghaidy H (2014) Interdisciplinary project-based learning: an online wiki experience in instructor education. Technol Pedagog Educ 24(3):339–355. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1080/1475939X.2014.899510
                  
                "); Yueh et al., [2015](/articles/s41599-024-02813-z#ref-CR49 "Yueh HP, Lin YL, Lin W (2015) Fostering interdisciplinary learning in a smart living technology course through a PBL approach. Int J Eng Educ 31(1):220–228")). Emphasizing teamwork and collaborative learning in interdisciplinary project teams can improve effective communication and problem-solving skills and prepare students for future community engagement and development in the real world. These benefits highlight the necessity, relevance, and importance of IPBL for interdisciplinary integration. Therefore, IPBL is considered an important model for cultivating relevant skills for students aiming to find their ideal professions (Hsu and Shiue, [2018](/articles/s41599-024-02813-z#ref-CR16 "Hsu YC, Shiue YM (2018) Exploring the influence of using collaborative tools on the community of inquiry in an interdisciplinary project-based learning context. EURASIA J Math\ Sci Technol Educ 14(3):933–945. 
                  https://doi.org/10.12973/ejmste/81149
                  
                ")).

However, these PBL learning modes have limitations in meeting students’ learning needs for engagement with increasingly broad and complex challenges. A new emerging interdisciplinary inquiry-based learning approach: design-based learning (DBL) was proposed (Yang et al., [2018](/articles/s41599-024-02813-z#ref-CR47 "Yang C-Y, Kang S-C, Chen Y-F, Lin C-Y, Leng E-S, Lin Y-H (2018) Using “design-based learning” as preliminary foundation of smart aging design course. Chin J Sci Educ 26(S):399–418. 
                  https://doi.org/10.6173/CJSE.201812/SP_26.0002
                  
                ")). DBL was initially proposed by D. Nelson, and its concept is deeply influenced by J. Dewey’s philosophy, emphasizing learning through practical operation and action. It incorporates design concepts such as prototypes into the curriculum, innovating teaching methods to provide students with an integrated learning experience (Nelson, [2004](/articles/s41599-024-02813-z#ref-CR30 "Nelson D (2004) Design based learning delivers required standards in all subjects, K-12. Retrieved from California State Polytechnic University, Pomona Web site: 
                  https://www.csupomona.edu/~dnelson/documents/jis_vol17_fall04.doc
                  
                ")). It combines “design thinking” and “design practice”, allowing students to explore and solve real-life problems through reflective learning processes and hands-on doing (Mehalik and Schunn, [2006](/articles/s41599-024-02813-z#ref-CR25 "Mehalik MM, Schunn C (2006) What constitutes good design? A review of empirical studies of design processes. Int J Eng Educ 22(3):519–532")). Design thinking (DT) is an iterative, human-centered approach to solving complex problems that have gained considerate popularity in business, education, medicine, etc. (Dukala et al., [2023](/articles/s41599-024-02813-z#ref-CR10 "Dukala K, Pyrkosz-Pacyna J, Czarny R (2023) DTMethod: a new evidence-based design thinking methodology for effective teamwork. Sustainability 15(4187):1–4. 
                  https://doi.org/10.3390/su15054187
                  
                "); Li and Zhan, [2022](/articles/s41599-024-02813-z#ref-CR20 "Li T, Zhan Z (2022) A systematic review on design thinking integrated learning in K-12 education. Appl Sci 12(8077):1–34. 
                  https://doi.org/10.3390/app12168077
                  
                "); McLaughlin et al., [2022](/articles/s41599-024-02813-z#ref-CR24 "McLaughlin JE, Chen E, Lake D, Guo W, Skywark ER, Chernik A et al. (2022) Design thinking teaching and learning in higher education: experiences across four universities. PLoS ONE 17(3):e0265902. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0265902
                  
                "); Skywark et al., [2022](/articles/s41599-024-02813-z#ref-CR34 "Skywark ER, Chen E, Jagannathan V (2022) Using the design thinking process to co-create a new, interdisciplinary design thinking course to train 21st century graduate students. Front Public Health 9(777869):1–10. 
                  https://doi.org/10.3389/fpubh.2021.777869
                  
                ")). DT is frequently defined as the application of a designer’s sensitivity and techniques to align the requirements of individuals with what is technologically possible and what a feasible business strategy can transform into customer value and market opportunity (Brown, [2008](/articles/s41599-024-02813-z#ref-CR3 "Brown T (2008) Design thinking. Harvard Bus Rev 2008:84–92"), p. 2). In the practical tasks of DBL, students are required to construct tangible artifacts, fostering higher-order thinking abilities and demonstrating creative, design, and decision-making thinking (Chen and Yang, [2020](/articles/s41599-024-02813-z#ref-CR8 "Chen W, Yang T (2020) Competency-based curriculum design: The application of \"design-based learning.\" J. Spec. Educ 32, 10–23")). Compared to PBL and PjBL, DBL places a greater emphasis on humanism, particularly focusing on the human-based problem-solving process, prototype testing, and iterative refinement stages (Wang, [2023](/articles/s41599-024-02813-z#ref-CR42 "Wang C-C (2023) Enabling interdisciplinary competence-based teaching practices through design thinking. Taiwan Nat Sci 42(3):6–13. 
                  https://doi.org/10.29879/TNS
                  
                "); Yang et al., [2018](/articles/s41599-024-02813-z#ref-CR47 "Yang C-Y, Kang S-C, Chen Y-F, Lin C-Y, Leng E-S, Lin Y-H (2018) Using “design-based learning” as preliminary foundation of smart aging design course. Chin J Sci Educ 26(S):399–418. 
                  https://doi.org/10.6173/CJSE.201812/SP_26.0002
                  
                ")). Therefore, using DBL as the core of interdisciplinary curriculum design and teaching is expected to be a concrete, feasible, and essential pedagogical strategy in the future.

### Challenges and strategies in implementing interdisciplinary curriculum

Some interdisciplinary research has shown that implementing an interdisciplinary curriculum impacts instructors and learners significantly (Chang and Lin, [2019](/articles/s41599-024-02813-z#ref-CR6 "Chang C-Y, Lin C-C (2019) Promoting transdisciplinary learning in higher education: trends, myths, approaches and challenges. Curric Instr Q 22(2):31–48. 
                  https://doi.org/10.6384/CIQ.201904_22(2).0002
                  
                "); McLaughlin et al., [2022](/articles/s41599-024-02813-z#ref-CR24 "McLaughlin JE, Chen E, Lake D, Guo W, Skywark ER, Chernik A et al. (2022) Design thinking teaching and learning in higher education: experiences across four universities. PLoS ONE 17(3):e0265902. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0265902
                  
                "); Spelt et al., [2009](/articles/s41599-024-02813-z#ref-CR35 "Spelt EJH, Biemans HJA, Tobi H, Luning PA, Mulder M (2009) Teaching and learning in interdisciplinary higher education: a systematic review. Educ Psychol Rev 21:365–378. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1007/s10648-009-9113-z
                  
                ")). For instructors, interdisciplinary dialog enables them to learn from others and identify their weaker areas, leading to professional growth and the ability to integrate knowledge from multiple fields. These benefits enhance their teaching and curriculum planning abilities and contribute to guiding students in their thinking and collaboration with a view to solving problems. Several aspects need to be considered when developing an interdisciplinary curriculum, including assessing the appropriateness of the course content, balancing different fields, encouraging creativity, collaborating with external partners, and addressing real-world problems and social trends. When designing interdisciplinary project courses, instructors should focus on how to teach the required hard skills for the topic, and as students begin to communicate with others, their need for soft skills becomes apparent. If instructors do not provide sufficient support, communication issues may hinder the development of hard skills. There is no need to distinguish between cultivating soft and hard skills during the learning process, as they are mutually beneficial.

However, without instructors providing scaffolding for these interactive processes, students may not automatically develop soft or hard skills in interdisciplinary project courses. Therefore, instructors engaging in interdisciplinary teaching must carefully consider how to offer guidance and scaffolding to students aiming to develop both hard and soft skills (Vogler et al., [2018](/articles/s41599-024-02813-z#ref-CR41 "Vogler JS, Thompson P, Davis DW, Mayfield BE, Finley PM, Yasseri D (2018) The hard work of soft skills: augmenting the project-based learning experience with interdisciplinary teamwork. Instr Sci 46:457–488. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1007/s11251-017-9438-9
                  
                ")). For learners, interdisciplinary learning provides opportunities for exploration from multiple perspectives, utilizing knowledge and skills from their field and collaborating with students from other fields. Interdisciplinary approaches prioritize the development of higher-order thinking skills, such as analysis, application, generalization, and forming meaningful connections between different disciplines. Although these approaches may be less effective than traditional methods in building in-depth knowledge of a single subject, they offer unique benefits that cannot be achieved through a single-subject focus (Ivanitskaya et al., [2002](/articles/s41599-024-02813-z#ref-CR17 "Ivanitskaya L, Clark D, Montgomery G, Primeau R (2002) Interdisciplinary learning: process and outcomes. Innov High Educ 27(2):95–111. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1023/A:1021105309984
                  
                ")). Students who have received interdisciplinary education training, influenced by diverse ways of thinking in different fields, will have more diverse learning styles and thinking patterns (Chen et al., [2017](/articles/s41599-024-02813-z#ref-CR7 "Chen L-C, Wang T-H, Chiu F-Y, Shen S-Y, Zeng M (2017) Developing the interdisciplinary integration-based core competencies scale: a case study of maternal–infant services curriculum. Chin J Sci Edu 25(2):143–168. 
                  https://doi.org/10.6173/CJSE.2017.2502.03
                  
                ")).

In addition, implementing an interdisciplinary curriculum poses significant challenges. Instructors in higher education institutions often lack training in interdisciplinary or transdisciplinary education, viewing interdisciplinary project activities as intimidating, especially when they cut across different classes and disciplines (Vogler et al., [2018](/articles/s41599-024-02813-z#ref-CR41 "Vogler JS, Thompson P, Davis DW, Mayfield BE, Finley PM, Yasseri D (2018) The hard work of soft skills: augmenting the project-based learning experience with interdisciplinary teamwork. Instr Sci 46:457–488. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1007/s11251-017-9438-9
                  
                ")). Bridging cognitive and ideational gaps for effective communication among students from diverse fields in the classroom proves challenging (Tsai, [2014](/articles/s41599-024-02813-z#ref-CR40 "Tsai M-L (2014) How to facilitate learning of interdisciplinary

enriched_points.expanded_content: Academic disciplines often operate in isolation, limiting collaboration and sharing of knowledge, which can hinder innovation and the application of diverse perspectives in problem-solving.

Integrating design thinking across disciplines is challenging due to differences in terminology, methodologies, and cognitive approaches, which can lead to misunderstandings and ineffective collaboration.

There is a growing demand for interdisciplinary courses that combine insights from various fields, enabling students to tackle complex real-world problems through collaborative and innovative approaches.
